data.title: Taylor Wimpey: Limited Short-Term Growth Potential
data.description:
U.K. housebuilder Taylor Wimpey (OTCPK:TWODF) this week released its annual results for 2024.

I last covered the name in my 2020 “sell” piece Taylor Wimpey: Capital Raise Reflects Challenging Market, since when it is down 18% (although, in fairness, at one point between the publication of that piece and now it was up 31%).

Based on a broadly underwhelming 2024 and limited short-term drivers for business upside, I maintain my “sell” rating.

The U.K. Housing Market is Not Looking Great

In theory, housebuilding should be a booming sector in Britain right now. There is too little supply to meet demand, the government (as its predecessor did) have pledged massive building, and mortgage availability is not a chokepoint across the board.

However, things aren’t looking anywhere near as rosy as they should be. We’ve seen that from a number of housebuilders in recent months. Taylor Wimpey’s trading statement last month was therefore relatively upbeat.

It said it started this year with a stronger order book than at the same point last year and said enquiry levels have been “encouraging”. But it also pointedly said it was too early to gauge customer behaviour and pointed to uncertainty about mortgage rates and their impact on affordability.

It also pointed to expected cost inflation, including due to higher labour costs following the socialist U.K. government’s Budget introducing higher tax contributions from employers.

So, Taylor Wimpey left things open as to how this year might turn out – but it certainly dangled some reasons it could be tough, following a wider theme in the sector.

2024 was not Great for Taylor Wimpey

Taylor Wimpey had managed expectations for 2024 well so the results did not come as much of a surprise.

Still, they were nothing to write home about. Revenue fell, net cash fell and profit before tax slumped almost a third.

Group completions (including JVs) fell 2%, while the U.K. average selling price on private completions fell 4%. That is a red flag for me, as declining selling prices in an industry with rising costs is not a healthy combination, as the pre-tax profit decline shows.

The dividend per share fell 1%. While in line with the company’s dividend policy (two equal ordinary dividends per year that together return c. 7.5% of net assets to shareholders), it is still the first decline in the total annual dividend since before the pandemic, which I do not see as an attractive feature of the investment case.

Outlook for 2025 Suggests Limited Growth Potential

2024 did have challenges for the U.K. market, including mortgage availability and so I cut Taylor Wimpey some slack for that. Nonetheless, the year was relatively benign yet Taylor Wimpey’s performance was underwhelming in my view.

Looking ahead to the current year, the company talked of “robust”, “healthy”, “encouraging” engagement and “good levels of demand”, suggesting that the stronger end it saw to 2024 has carried over to this year.

Its total order book excluding JVs as of this week was £2.3bn, 16% ahead of this point last year.

It said it currently expects the year to come in line with market expectations. That would be 10,400 - 10,800 UK completions excluding JVs. Even at the top end of that range, it is very slightly beneath 2023 levels.

At the lower end, it would be a further decline from 2024, but most of the range foresees some uplift versus 2024.

In short, 2025 looks like it will be fairly similar to last year in terms of volumes unless the housing market or mortgage availability suddenly get worse, both of which I see as risks. What that volume means for revenues and profits is unclear. Last year’s weaker selling prices were not encouraging. Taylor Wimpey expects low single digit build cost inflation for the year, which may eat into margins. But the bigger risk I see is whether it can sustain selling prices.

Valuation Still Looks a Bit High

Currently, Taylor Wimpey trades on a P/E ratio of 16, with a market capitalization of£4bn.

I continue to see that as too high. The share has fallen 20% in a year and the business outlook is middling.

Taylor Wimpey is not the only British homebuilder with what I think is a high valuation Bellway (OTCPK:BLWYF) sells on a P/E ratio of 21 and Persimmon (OTCPK:PSMMF) on 14. Those are markedly higher than, say, Berkeley (OTCPK:BKGFY) on 10 or Vistry (OTCPK:BVHMF) on 8.

For a notoriously cyclical industry facing an uncertain economy I think a mid-teens P/E ratio is high for a business that expects weak or zero volume growth this year and saw profits fall by a third last year.

That said, Taylor Wimpey’s dividend yield of 8.6% is highly attractive. Whether it is maintained, though, depends on business performance. From a valuation perspective, I see it as a bonus but not a standalone reason to buy in this market. Accordingly I maintain my “sell” rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: Sell Before The Oil Becomes Irrelevant
data.description:
The age of oil producers dictating the prices for oil is likely coming to an end because of the increased supply across different parts of the world, and ExxonMobil (NYSE:XOM) could become one of the biggest losers of this new geopolitical reality. The U.S. oil production is already at record highs and is likely to increase under the Trump administration. Also, OPEC+ might soon change its production policy to regain the lost market share, which could prompt a depreciation of global oil prices on which ExxonMobil’s business greatly relies. We believe that the company’s best times are behind it and after years of record profits, the party is likely coming to an end.

This Is A Buyer’s Market Now

ExxonMobil is highly reliant on the prices for oil and its business along with the performance of its shares closely correlate with the prices for oil on the global market. The chart below shows that ExxonMobil’s shares appreciated in 2021 thanks to the Covid-related recovery and in 2022 because of the Russian invasion of Ukraine, which also pushed oil prices to record levels. Once the supply-related disruptions following the war on the European continent were mostly neutralized in late 2022 and early 2023, the prices for oil stabilized, and ExxonMobil’s shares got stuck mostly in the range between $100 per share and $125 per share.

Going forward, we think that ExxonMobil’s upside is almost non-existent as there is an indication that the oil prices will decrease in the following years, which will more than likely drag the company’s shares down as well. In the latest quarter, ExxonMobil already reported a 1.1% Y/Y decline in revenues, which was also below expectations by a few billion dollars, and a further decline in revenues in the following years is more than likely at this point.

One of the reasons for pessimism is the record production of oil in the United States, which prevents a rapid rise in the price of oil. Thanks to the shale revolution that occurred in the last couple of decades, the United States tapped the previously unavailable oil reserves and currently pumps more oil than ever. At the same time, the EIA expects non-OPEC production to accelerate in the following years, while the IEA forecasts an increase in supply that will outstrip the demand. This would give more leverage to the buyers rather than to the sellers of oil.

In such an environment, the OPEC+ cartel will likely be required to change its current production policy. While OPEC+ decided to cut the production of oil in 2022 to keep the prices relatively high, the rise in non-OPEC+ supply was able to somewhat offset the loss of OPEC+ supply. This resulted in the decrease in market share of OPEC+ countries as buyers began to purchase more oil from non-OPEC+ countries. That is why it’s more than likely that OPEC+ will start increasing its supply starting in April to try and regain the lost market share. This is certainly a negative development for ExxonMobil, as the competition in the global oil market is about to increase once again and likely drive the oil prices even lower.

In addition to that, the weak demand for fuel from China at large because of the rise of electric vehicles along with the weak consumer sentiment in the United States make it nearly impossible for oil prices to aggressively increase in the foreseeable future.

Because of all of those and other developments, the EIA now expects a decline in oil prices in 2025 and 2026, which is not a good thing for ExxonMobil’s price-dependent business. As for the longer term, the IEA now forecasts that the demand for oil for different purposes could even peak in the following years.

This is a major issue for ExxonMobil in our opinion. The company is already expected to see a decline in revenues and earnings in 2025. Considering the latest downward revisions and the recent trends in the oil market, there’s a possibility that we’ll see a decline in sales and earnings in 2026 as well. Because of this, we believe that ExxonMobil’s stock doesn’t offer any upside to investors at the current price.

The Real Value of ExxonMobil

The forecasted decline of oil prices in the following years makes ExxonMobil an unattractive investment in our opinion. We created a valuation model, to see how big of a downside its shares represent if the oil prices continue to decline.

The perpetual growth rate in our model is 3%, which we use most of the time in our valuation models on other companies. The forecasted period in our model covers the next five years, and the long-term debt and cash data has been taken from ExxonMobil’s recent earnings report.

The discount rate in our model is 6.83%. We figured it out by calculating ExxonMobil’s after-tax cost of debt and cost of equity. In the cost of debt calculation, we mostly used ExxonMobil’s TTM data. For the cost of equity calculation, we used the risk-free rate of 4.30%, a beta of 0.88, and the market return rate of 7.80%. Once we figured out the cost of debt and equity, we then weighted them against each other to arrive at our discount rate.

In the forecast table below, we expect a gradual decline in sales in the next few years as the oil prices are expected to decrease mostly thanks to the increase in supply across the globe.

Earnings are also expected to decline as a result of lower oil prices that result in a lower margin. As such, we decreased the company’s EBIT margin from 12.9% in 2024 to 12.17% in 2025 and beyond, as it is the current TTM margin right now.

The tax rate in our model for the following years is 25%, which is not far away from the rate that the company had in recent years. The D&A in the model remains at 6.6% of total sales, and the change in net working capital as a percentage of sales also remains the same as in the last year.

The CapEx in the model is increased and is also similar to the management’s expectations. In 2025, CapEx is expected to be in the range between $27 billion and $29 billion, while from 2026, and later, it’s expected to be in the range between $28 billion to $33 billion.

Under all of those assumptions, we arrive at ExxonMobil’s enterprise value of $323.99 billion and the equity value of $312.39 billion. That translates to an intrinsic value of $70.61 per share, which implies a ~35% downside from the current price.

Our model shows that ExxonMobil’s shares represent a downside, and that is one of the main reasons why we believe that the company is not an attractive long-term investment.

Risks to Our Bearish Thesis

Some positive developments can undermine our thesis. While the decline in oil prices in the long run is almost certain, ExxonMobil still can find a way to improve its performance if it manages to decrease its breakeven price. The company believes that its new projects in Guyana and the Permian Basin could drive ExxonMobil’s breakeven price to $30 per barrel by the end of the decade, which should help it better adapt to the new reality.

At the same time, the potential improvement of China’s economy following the increase in trade along with the end of the Russian war against Ukraine could also give a short-term boost to oil prices, which could help ExxonMobil perform better than we expect.

Final Thoughts

ExxonMobil made great returns in recent years, but the party is likely coming to an end. The abundance of oil thanks to the increased supply across the globe along with the potential change of OPEC+ production policy is likely to create an environment where oil producers no longer dictate the global oil prices. While ExxonMobil still has some growth catalysts on its side, we believe that it won’t be enough to significantly boost the business and justify the current stock price. That is why we are rating ExxonMobil as a SELL and don’t see any upside to its shares.

data.title: Boeing's Valuation Can't Be Justified
data.description:
With an enterprise value of $164.32 billion as of this writing, Boeing (NYSE:BA) is certainly a giant in the stock market. But more importantly, it is a behemoth in the aerospace and defense markets. However, those who follow the business closely understand that it has found itself in a difficult financial position for several years now. Last October, I wrote an article about a massive capital raise that the company had embarked on worth $25 billion. At that time, I mentioned that while this does help to plug an important hole, it didn't solve the company's problems moving forward. The fact of the matter is that the business continues to struggle to generate positive earnings and cash flow. And normally, a business in this financial condition would justify a bearish outlook from me.

This is especially true when you consider how weak the most recent financial performance of the company has been. However, the one time nature of some of this weakness needs to be factored in. Looking at the picture now, after the stock had risen by 14.5% at a time when the S&P 500 is up only 3.2%, I do think that even more caution is warranted than what was warranted back then. Even though the balance sheet of the company is now better than it was, I really don't see much upside. In fact, I will even go so far as to say that downgrading it from a 'hold' to a soft 'sell' is appropriate at this time.

Upside is limited at best

Fundamentally speaking, recent financial performance provided by the management team at Boeing has been nothing shy of weak. Take the final quarter of the firm's 2024 fiscal year as an example. During that time, revenue for the business was $15.24 billion. That was down considerably from the $22.02 billion the company reported one year earlier. This weakness can be seen across two of the three operating segments for the company. The first of these is the Commercial Airplanes segment, commonly referred to by management as the BCA segment. As the name suggests, this is a part of the company that produces and sells commercial jet aircraft to the firm's customers. Examples here include its 737, 767, 770, and 787. The firm also has other programs in place such as its 777X program.

Year over year, revenue for this unit dropped significantly from $10.48 billion to $4.76 billion. This is a decline of 54.6% and can be attributed to a drop in deliveries from 157 aircraft to 57 aircraft. Now, to be fair, this pain was driven by a couple of factors that should be one time in nature. The primary culprit seems to have been a work stoppage involving the IAM (the International Association of Machinists and Aerospace Workers). For nearly two months last year, workers at facilities in Washington, Oregon, and California, stopped working. This 53 day halt to production significantly decreased overall output. By early November, a new contract had been agreed upon. So this should not be something that repeats itself for quite some time.

There was also pain in involving the company's Defense, Space & Security segment, which management typically refers to as the firm's BDS segment. This is the part of the company that is focused on military aircraft and weapons, not to mention other technologies that it provides for governments. Examples here include the development of strategic defense and intelligence systems like missile and defense systems, as well as surveillance and reconnaissance technology, cyber and information solutions, satellite systems, and more. Year over year, this segment saw revenue drop 19.8% from $6.75 billion to $5.41 billion. This can be attributed to a decline in the number of deliveries from 54 units to 36 units.

The only growth area for the business was its Global Services segment, which management refers to as the BGS segment. According to management, this unit provides customers, both on the commercial side and on the defense side, with important services such as supply chain and logistics management, engineering, maintenance, modifications, the provision of spare parts, pilot and maintenance training systems, data analytics, and more. Thanks to higher commercial volume and a favorable change in order mix, revenue for it grew by 5.6% from $4.85 billion to $5.12 billion.

Unfortunately, the bottom line for the business has worsened as well. The firm went from a net loss of $23 million to a net loss of $3.92 billion. Operating cash flow went from $3.38 billion to negative $3.45 billion. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $965 million to negative $1.62 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the firm contracted from $1.05 billion to negative $3.40 billion.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2024 as a whole relative to 2023. In large part because of the weakness experienced during the final quarter of the year, revenue, profits, and cash flows, all worsened without exception. And in the chart below, you can see what the picture has looked like for the business from a margin perspective. It has gotten to the point where even the cost of producing revenue is higher than the revenue itself. When you add on top of this growing general and administrative costs, growing research and development costs, and rising interest expense, all relative to revenue, the picture just looks bad.

As I've already mentioned, you do have to cut the company some slack because of the work stoppage that it experienced last year. After all, there are some positives for investors to focus on. In the chart below, for instance, you can see historical revenue and backlog for the company for the last five completed fiscal years. From 2020 through 2024, backlog for the company surged an impressive 43.5%.

For all the company's troubles, this means that customers are continuing to use the company for the products it offers. Furthermore, as the chart below illustrates, deliveries, at least when it comes to the Commercial Airplanes segment, rose consistently year after year from 2020 through 2023. This signifies to me that this is a business that has no problem landing revenue. The problem, instead, involves its bottom line.

In my last article about the company, one thing that I pointed out was that it continues to generate significant and growing losses associated with its five largest fixed price contracts. These are major contracts that the company has. To be clear, in 2024, about 54% of the company's BDS revenue came from fixed price contracts. And when it came to the BGS segment, that number was 63%. However, under the BDS segment, its five largest contracts resulted in losses for the year of $5.01 billion. Almost half of this was from the KC-46A Tanker project, with the company incurring a loss of $2 billion. The T-7A Red Hawk project cost it another $1.77 billion. To put this in perspective, in 2023, losses associated with its five largest fixed price contracts for this segment totaled $1.59 billion.

In addition to this being a pain point, there is also the fact that, historically speaking, Boeing has really struggled from a profitability perspective. It almost seems like the company is willing to accept new projects but unable have a good idea of what those ultimate costs might be. A move away from fixed price contracts would certainly be a positive for shareholders. To see what I mean about the overall financial performance of the business in recent years, we need only look at the chart below. In it, you can see that adjusted operating cash flows have been negative in three of the last five fiscal years. Meanwhile, EBITDA has been negative in two of the last five. However, those negative readings for EBITDA have been so large that the metric, for the last five years, has been negative to the tune of $8.69 billion.

For most companies, except for the highest quality or the fastest growing, a general rule of thumb that I like to rely on is to say that they should probably be worth no more than 10 or 15 times cash flow. In the chart below, you can see how much adjusted operating cash flow that Boeing would need to generate an order would be trading at either of these multiples on a price to adjusted operating cash flow basis. The chart also shows the same thing with EBITDA on an EV to EBITDA basis. These numbers are drastically higher than what the company has generated in any of the last five fiscal years.

*$ in Billions

This alone makes it a difficult prospect to get behind. But when you add on top of this the fact that the stock is up 20.9% since I last wrote about it and reaffirmed it as a 'hold', I think that downgrading it to a 'sell' is a foregone conclusion. This is not to say that the company doesn't have potential if management can get its house in order. In the chart below, you can see a few ways to value the company today. I normally like to value a company based on cash flows. But given the circumstances, this just is not possible at this time. Instead, however, the chart shows how the stock is valued on a price to sales basis, an EV to sales basis, a price to backlog basis, and an EV to backlog basis. Interestingly, the firm seems to be trading in a pretty narrow range relative to revenue. But when we compare the valuation of the company to backlog, it has gotten considerably cheaper. If we can see that management is starting to turn the ship around, and if that backlog starts to become profitable, it's not insane to think that there might be some upside here. But for now, the bullish case is fairly weak.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I do think it's finally time to downgrade Boeing from a 'hold' to a 'sell'. This is not based so much on the most recent financial performance that I know to be a temporary blip on the radar. Rather, it's based on the fact that overall performance for an extended period of time has been disappointing. Cash flows are nowhere near they should be and I don't see any reason why further upside from this point would be warranted

data.title: Chemtrade Logistics: 2025 Guidance Confirms Sub-50% Payout Ratio
data.description:
Introduction

I have been following Chemtrade Logistics ( CHE.UN:CA) (OTC:CGIFF) for several years now. As mentioned in my previous articles, Chemtrade is a company focusing on two specific divisions: Sulphur and water chemicals and electro chemicals. I used to have a long position in one of the company’s series of convertible debentures but recently sold the debentures and initiated a long position in the common shares as the dividend yield of almost 7% in combination with a payout ratio of less than 50% is quite appealing.

This article is meant as an update to previous coverage. For a better understanding of what Chemtrade does and what its core business is, I’d like to refer you to this older article.

2024 was a good year and this resulted in a distribution hike

Chemtrade’s appeal should be based on the distributable cash flow result. This basically represents the net sustaining free cash flow result as it excludes the growth investments incurred by the company.

As you can see below, Chemtrade reported an operating cash flow of almost exactly C$100M in the final quarter of last year. It also incurred almost C$36.1M in sustaining capex which was relatively high given the full-year sustaining capex of less than C$105M.

This resulted in a total DCF of C$39.5M or C$0.33 per unit. Looking at the full-year result, the DCF was C$213M, resulting in a net result of C$1.80 per share. A strong result, despite having reached a peak result in 2023 on the back of massive tailwinds in the industry.

Thanks to the strong results and rapidly improving balance sheet, Chemtrade also increased its monthly distribution by approximately 5% to C$0.0575 per unit per month. This results in a full-year distribution of C$0.69 per share for a yield of almost 7%. Given the strong DCF performance in 2024 and the robust outlook for 2025, the generous distribution in combination with a low payout ratio makes the stock quite attractive.

It's also worth mentioning that Chemtrade continues to buy back its own stock. The total unit count increased in 2024, mainly because a convertible debenture was converted into stock. Despite this, the Chemtrade kept the "dilution" limited as it repurchased in excess of 5.1M units.

And the buyback plans are still in full effect. In the first eight weeks of the year, Chemtrade repurchased just over 2.7M units, reducing the unit count to just over 116M units. As one series of the convertible debentures has a conversion price that’s below the current share price we may see additional dilution, but as that specific debenture has 2.5 more years to go, it’s a 2027 problem.

But while the unit count may increase again, the company will also save about C$8M on interest expenses and convert C$130M from debt into equity, making the balance sheet even safer. So a conversion would be relatively neutral and not necessarily an adverse effect.

The total net debt (including leases) at the end of FY 2024 was C$864M, representing a multiple of just 1.8 times the EBITDA. Excluding lease liabilities and lease payments, the net financial debt is C$658M, representing just 1.7 times the anticipated EBITDA for 2025 (and 1.6 times the LTM lease-adjusted EBITDA).

The outlook for 2025 remains promising

The company also reconfirmed its previously published guidance for this year. As you can see below, Chemtrade expects to generate an adjusted EBITDA of C$430-460M. Using the midpoint of C$445M and using the midpoint of the sustaining capex (C$110M), the lease payments (C$70M), interest payments (C$50M) and cash taxes (C$50M), the distributable cash flow will come in at around C$165M.

Considering the current unit count is approximately 116.3M units, this represents a distributable cash flow of approximately C$1.42 per unit. This means the stock is currently trading at just 7 times DCF while the increased distribution remains very well covered with an anticipated payout ratio of just 48.6%. This also means that Chemtrade will be able to cover the growth capex with the post-dividend cash flow.

Note: The anticipated DCF does not include the impact of a C$28M cash payment to settle an old lawsuit. That's a non-recurring item, and I think Chemtrade should be judged based on its underlying and recurring results.

Investment thesis

While Chemtrade’s guidance implies a weaker result in 2025 than in 2024, this is not unexpected as the company is coming off a cyclical high in 2023 when its DCF came in at an extremely impressive C$284M. This allowed Chemtrade to rapidly clean up its balance sheet, putting the company on solid footing for the future.

I have started to build a long position in the company's common shares and I plan to continue to add to this position over the next few weeks and quarters.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Anheuser-Busch InBev's Reversal Is Here: Double Digits Upside Potential Ahead
data.description:
BUD Remains Attractively Valued - Offering Opportunistic Investors With The Dual Pronged Returns

We previously covered Anheuser-Busch InBev SA/NV (BUD) (OTCPK:BUDFF) in January 2025, discussing why the stock had been sold off then, attributed to the numerous headwinds across FX devaluation, declining alcohol volumes sold on YoY basis, and potential cancer label.

Even so, we had rated the stock as an opportunistic buy near pandemic lows, attributed to its expanding adj EBITDA, promising consensus estimates, and the stock's oversold status, with it potentially triggering a rich capital appreciation prospect upon market sentiment reversal.

BUD 1Y Stock Price

Since then, BUD has bounced from its all-time support levels of $41s as expected as it also charts an impressive +26.3% return within the past few weeks, with the same outperformance also observed in its peers in varying degrees.

This is a surprising development indeed, given the intensified tariff war on alcohol and aluminum, one that may directly impact the breweries' intermediate term prospects no matter the 30-day suspension.

This is especially since the new US administration is likely to proceed with its import tariffs on Mexico (no matter the uncertain deadline), one that may trigger headwinds to BUD's top/bottom-lines, attributed to Corona beer's FY2024 global volume growth by +9.4% YoY and immense popularity in the country:

In its home market of Mexico, Corona is the #1 brand in the industry and volumes grew by mid-single-digits... Corona sells on average at 20% premium to the nearest competitor. In recognition of this past performance and its future potential, Corona was named the most valuable beer brand in the world in 2024. (Seeking Alpha)

While BUD does not break down its sales by countries, it goes without saying that the North American segment (including Canada and Mexico) comprises a significant portion of its volumes sold at 14.9% in FY2024 (-0.4 points YoY), top-line performance at 24.5% (+0.2 points YoY), and adj EBITDA at 22.8% (-0.8 points YoY).

These reasons are also why we believed that the recent rally may have occurred overly fast and furious, especially since we may see an uncertain FY2025 performance ahead, pending further policy clarity.

For now, part of the stock price recovery tailwinds may be attributed to BUD's double beat FQ4'24 performance, with revenues of $14.84B (-1.3% QoQ/ +2.5% YoY), adj EBITDA margins of 35.3% (-0.7 points QoQ/ +1.6 YoY), and adj EPS of $0.88 (+2.3% QoQ/ +7.3% YoY).

This is on top of the successful premiumization strategy, as observed in the higher revenues per hl by +4.3% YoY and the richer gross profit margins by +1.4 points YoY, despite the -2% YoY moderation in its volumes sold in FY2024.

Despite the secular decline in the alcohol industry, BUD has demonstrated great initiatives to remain relevant indeed, as observed in the Beyond Beer segment's growth by "low single digits led by double-digit growth in key brands such as Cutwater, Nütrl and Brutal Fruit" and in the no-alcohol beer segment growth by "low-twenties" in FY2024.

Combined with the "production cost efficiencies and disciplined overhead management" along with successful D2C strategy, it is unsurprising that the company has reported richer bottom-lines and drastically expanded Free Cash Flow generation at $11.32B in FY2024 (+28.6% YoY).

These efforts have directly been accretive to BUD's balance sheet health at net debts of -$60.6B (-10.3% YoY) and lower net debt to EBITDA ratio of 2.89x, compared to 3.38x reported a year ago, with it underscoring why the management leverage target of 2x does not appear to be overly aggressive.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

For now, BUD has already guided a promising FY2025 adj EBITDA growth at +6% in the midpoint, "in line with our medium-term outlook" with it building upon the robust FY2024 adj EBITDA growth to $20.95B (+8.2% YoY) and adj EBITDA margin expansion to 35.1% (+1.5 points YoY).

These reasons are likely why BUD is likely to outperform the consensus estimates of top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +3.5%/ +4.4% through FY2027, while accelerating from the 5Y historical growth at +2.7%/ -2.9% and 10Y growth at +2.4%/ -4.2%, respectively, underscoring its renewed growth opportunity ahead.

BUD Valuations

These reasons are also why we believe that BUD remains cheap at FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 8.96x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 16.47x, compared to its 1Y mean of 9.05x/ 17.01x, 5Y mean of 10.54x/ 20.73x, and 10Y mean of 11.78x/ 20.88x, respectively, despite the notable upgrade from the prior article levels of 8.02x/ 13.85x.

Based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 16.47x and the projected expansion in its adj EPS at a CAGR of +13% through FY2027, we believe that BUD's estimated FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.26x remains reasonable compared to its brewery peers, including Constellation Brands (STZ) at 1.45x, The Boston Beer Company (SAM) at 1.17x, and Molson Coors Beverage (TAP) at 1.74x.

These developments imply BUD's successful reversal from the challenging past five years and the Dylan Mulvaney fiasco, with it offering interested investors with an attractive investment thesis.

So, Is BUD Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

BUD 15Y Stock Price

For now, BUD has already bounced from the established floor of $40s ranges as it also break-outs from the prior resistance levels of $52s while currently retesting the next resistance levels/200 day moving averages of $58s.

Even so, it is apparent that the stock remains cheap compared to our prior fair value estimate of $60.60 offered in our last article, based on the LTM adj EPS of $3.45 ending FQ3'24 (+9.5% sequentially) and the prior 1Y P/E non-GAAP mean of 17.58x.

Based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $5.09, there remains an extremely rich upside potential of +52.2% to our long-term price target of $89.40 as well, despite the recent rally.

Combined with the raised FY2024 dividend payout to €1 per share (+22% YoY), we believe that BUD's investment thesis across capital appreciation and dividend incomes remain compelling, resulting in our reiterated Buy rating.

Risk Warning

For now, given BUD's massive rally since the recent bottom, we urge interested investors to observe the price movement for a little longer before adding.

Our caution is attributed to the FX fluctuation and commodity cost headwinds, which have already contributed to the FY2024 bottom-line impacts by -2.9% and -2%, respectively.

Given the still sticky inflationary pressure, the Fed's hawkish commentary, and the potential impacts from the intensified tariff war, we believe that FY2025 may bring forth moderate uncertainties, with the macroeconomic outlook and consumer spending patterns likely to remain impacted for a little longer.

Combined with the higher short interest volume by +27.4% on a YoY basis, it is uncertain if the BUD stock may sustain its upward momentum ahead, barring which, we may see a near-term pullback to its 100-day moving averages of $54s ranges or a 50-day moving averages of $50s ranges, implying a potential downside of -6.8% or -14.8% from current levels.

data.title: Broadcom, CrowdStrike To Report Results As Investors Look To Jobs Report
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Investors will be focused on Friday’s jobs report in the U.S. for February. Purchasing managers data for February will be released on Monday from S&P Global and the Institute for Supply Management, while factory and durable goods order statistics for January will be released on Wednesday. Meanwhile, EU inflation data for last month will be out on Monday, and February retail sales data are due out on Thursday.

CrowdStrike (CRWD), Broadcom (AVGO), Target (TGT), Costco Wholesale (COST), and Kroger (KR) will report earnings next week.

Earnings spotlight: Monday, March 3 - Okta (OKTA), GitLab (GTLB), AST SpaceMobile (ASTS), California Resources (CRC), and Surgery Partners (SGRY). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, March 4 - CrowdStrike, Sea Ltd. (SE), Target, AutoZone (AZO), and Ross Stores (ROST). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, March 5 - Marvell Technology (MRVL), Veeva Systems (VEEV), Zscaler (ZS), Brown-Forman (BF.A)(BF.B), and Campbell’s (CPB). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, March 6 - Broadcom (AVGO), Costco Wholesale, Kroger, Burlington Stores (BURL), and BJ’s Wholesale Club Holdings (BJ).

Volatility watch: Intuitive Machines (LUNR) and Capricor Therapeutics (CAPR) have a high level of implied volatility based on options trading. SoundHound AI (SOUN) and Aehr Test Systems (AEHR) head into the new week with a high level of short interest outstanding on them. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Power Metals Corp (OTCQB:PWRMF), QVC Group (QVCGB), and China Yuchai (CYD). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Parsons (PSN) and Brazil Potash (GRO).

IPO watch: Kestra Medical Technologies (KMTS) is expected to price its IPO on Tuesday and begin trading on Wednesday. The lockup periods for Pheton Holdings (PTHL) and Powell Max (PMAX) expire on Tuesday.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include McDonald’s (MCD), Nike (NKE), Lockheed Martin (LMT), Sherwin Williams (SHW), and McKesson (MCK). Companies forecast to increase their quarterly dividends include American Express (AXP), from $0.70 to $0.82, Kadant (KAI), from $0.32 to $0.35, and Academy Sports and Outdoors (ASO), from $0.11 to $0.12. Read through some of the dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

Investor events: MWC Barcelona, also known as Mobile World Congress, will take place over four days starting Monday. The annual gathering brings together global leaders in the technology, mobile, and connectivity industries.

Broadcom earnings preview: Broadcom is scheduled to report its latest quarterly results on Thursday. The semiconductor company is expected to post EPS of $1.51 on revenue of $14.59B. Oppenheimer said in a note this week that it seems upside in the most recent quarter and current three-month period, driven by application-specific integrated circuits and networking. The firm said it expects new AI ASIC projects with OpenAI and Apple (AAPL) to contribute next year. “We believe AVGO has one of the most strategically and financially attractive business models in semiconductors,” Oppenheimer said.

CrowdStrike Holdings earnings preview: CrowdStrike is scheduled to report earnings on Tuesday. The cybersecurity firm is expected to post EPS of $0.86 on revenue of $1.03B. Truist said in a note earlier this month that the impact from the global outage last summer seems to be behind the company. “We believe the company has built in enough cushion in its guidance to account for delayed outbound prospecting, which has since fully resumed and is rising to pre-incident levels of responses,” Truist said. The note said CrowdStrike is continuing to benefit from recent “cyberattacks and is one of the best positioned security companies to benefit from vendor consolidation based on product breadth in our view.” Truist reiterated its Buy rating on CrowdStrike and raised its price target to $460 from $385.

data.title: USIG: Inflation Expectations Are Rising, Bearish On Duration Bets
data.description:
The iShares Broad USD Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (NASDAQ:USIG) is a rather high duration bond ETF, therefore exposed to rate risks quite considerably, particularly longer-term rates that are more volatile in the current environment given questions around the levels of inflation. We continue to worry in the US about the current administration's trade policies, while perhaps effective at tackling geopolitical issues, aggravates the sensitive area of inflation and inflation expectations. In terms of credit spreads, they remain at historical lows. While the economic engine isn't showing any alarming signs in the US, rates are still elevated compared to previous periods, and nominally high rates can still matter for more distressed parts of the economy, so historically low credit spreads compared to periods with much fewer economic issues strikes us as unusual.

USIG Breakdown

Firstly, duration is above 6.5 years, which constitutes the sensitivity factor to step changes in rates. With longer dated securities, particularly rates implied on the further part of the yield curve matter, since those are compounded for more periods and take a bigger toll on bond values through discounting. Most off the bonds are rated between A and BBB rated at almost 90% of USIG's allocations.

The yield curve currently looks like this. Note the significant changes in rates even in long-dated maturities, which means considerable changes in outlook around long-term rate expectations, which will be affected more by structural rather than transitory factors.

Remember, that inflation is both caused by current supply and demand dynamics, but also by expectations about future supply and demand dynamics, or even just expectations about other people's expectations about future supply and demand dynamics. Expectations of inflation create inflation even where there might not be structural scarcity or especially high demand due to inflation expectations affecting wage negotiations, which in turn causes companies to cover higher costs with their own price increases, which is definitionally inflation. Inflation expectations are the primary and most important cause of inflation and are tracked through surveys like those conducted by Michigan University. Inflation expectations can anchor inflation at a higher level, and are therefore that structural factor we mentioned earlier that affects long-term rates. For the last year inflation expectations have averaged around 2.7% or so. In the last three months it's risen a lot, to 3.3% and now the latest surveys showing 4.3% for the 1-year expectation. This is a powerful leading indicator for inflation, and just this fact alone has us eschewing long-duration bonds. Latest CPI data has also been above target once again at 3%, signaling reinflation, with comps also getting harder since the base includes a lower oil price and already deflated commodities after COVID-19.

The reason for the higher inflation expectations comes down to the consuming public's assessment around risks to prices of the goods they purchase due to Trumpflation. Tariffs have been pledged against neighbours, requiring them to come to the table on other issues like Fentanyl and the cartels. They have been threatened against the EU as well. All of this will be rather inflationary given how much the US imports. It has caused stocking activity, which is naturally inflationary and one of the initial inflation impulses in this inflation saga started during COVID-19.

On the other hand, an end to the war in Ukraine could be a bit disinflationary for the US, where traders have been betting on the end to sanctions. Europe may continue with sanctions on Russia, but that's not important for the US rate discussion.

Bottom Line

Mostly we are concerned about inflation expectations which are more important for long-term rates than any other factor, and the survey data shows that inflation is pointing up and not down, hence the Fed's wait and see stance.

The other thing, though less important, not playing in USIG's favour are the credit spreads, which are at historical lows. Credit spreads for BBB rated securities are almost half what they were in very easygoing periods like 2017. We think there are certainly more economic and credit risks in the complicated environment of today compared to that of 2017, though maturity walls have been softened, and the economy has really been resilient.

USIG's expense ratio is negligible at 0.04%, which is very solid among long-duration fixed income ETFs. A good expense ratio would be below 0.15% for long-dated fixed income ETFs, and USIG is way below that. But that's not a reason to pick it when it stands in the way of unpredictable risks around inflation expectations, linked meaningfully to not just economic policy but also Trump's foreign policy strategies where he is threatening other nations with tariffs unless geopolitical concessions are made.

data.title: Corporate U.S. Clean Energy Purchases Near 120 GW, Driven By AI Datacenter Boom
data.description:
The rise of AI has caused expectations for datacenter electricity demand - and by extension, overall power demand - to skyrocket since the unveiling of ChatGPT in the fall of 2022, prompting a rush toward clean energy purchases. Corporations in the US have contracted nearly 48 GW of additional clean energy capacity in the 12 months since our February 2024 update, with Big Tech driving most of the activity.

Key Findings

➤ Overall, tracked clean energy capacity contracted by corporate offtakers in the US jumped 66.4% between our 2024 and 2025 updates.

➤ The technology and web services sector led the surge, contributing nearly 92% of total new additions.

➤ Nuclear energy makes up 43% of the tracked 47.6-GW increase year over year.

➤ Corporate buyers contracted nearly 14 GW of US solar capacity in 2024, compared to about 2.2 GW of wind capacity.

➤ Texas continues to lead all US states in corporate-tied clean energy capacity, accounting for 27.6% of the tracked total, but Missouri, Pennsylvania and North Dakota experienced the largest annual increases.

➤ US offtakers account for more than 92% of tracked clean energy corporate procurement in the US, according to S&P Global Commodity Insights tracking. European corporations make up the bulk of the balance.

Tech companies pivot to nuclear

Faced with unprecedented load growth due to the rising need for energy-intensive AI datacenters, US tech companies dominated the corporate energy procurement market in the interval under review. Recent projections have datacenter energy consumption doubling to nearly 800 TWh by 2030 - more than triple the amount of electricity California consumed in 2023. To maintain pace with ambitious, self-imposed clean energy targets, datacenter companies were forced to take assertive action in energy procurement.

Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) and Brookfield Renewable Corp. (BEPC) announced one of the largest corporate renewable deals in history in May 2024, partnering to add 10.5 GW of renewable capacity to the grid in the US and Europe by 2030. Seven months later, Switch Inc. announced a partnership with advanced nuclear technology startup Oklo Inc. (OKLO) to build 12 GW of nuclear energy. Based on S&P Global Commodity Insights tracking, US tech companies signed clean energy deals across the US and the rest of the world totaling over 47 GW between February 2024 and February 2025.

Switch's announcement with Oklo was not the only nuclear deal announced in 2024. Nuclear generation resurged in 2024, given the technology's ability to service large loads without interruption and without emitting greenhouse gases. Over 20 GW worth of nuclear deals were announced since February 2024 - all to tech companies - though most of this capacity is unlikely to come online before 2030. See S&P Global Commodity Insights' 2025 US datacenters and nuclear energy report.

Solar remains the preferred technology for corporate offtakers securing clean energy capacity in the US, however, accounting for 49.1% of overall corporate-tied US clean-energy capacity tracked as of this report. Wind is a distant second, representing only 23.9% of the total.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Africa Oil Corp.'s Earnings Spell Opportunity
data.description:
Africa Oil Corporation (OTCMKTS: OTCPK:AOIFF) announced its 4Q 2024 earnings. Despite tough oil prices and some production issues in Nigeria, the company managed to more than justify its almost $600 million USD market cap. As we'll see throughout this article, the company will have a more impressive 2025, which will enable strong shareholder returns.

Results

The company managed to achieve respectable results based on its current asset operations.

The company had 17k barrels / day in full-year average daily working interest, slightly in line, but below management guidance. The company recorded 2024 FY CFFO of almost $270 million, just slightly below management guidance. Prime has $175 million in net debt, which will double when the company makes a pro forma merger, but it's more than manageable.

The company had 101% proved reserves with more than $1 billion in 2P NPV reserves. The company has almost 30 million barrels in reserves, and is producing roughly 5 million barrels / day as the asset continues to decline.

Financial Results

The company's financial results show a continued ability to perform.

The company's production declined slightly YoY but EBITDAX for the year went up along with FCF. Earning almost $200 million in attributable FCF, is enough to pay down the company's debt at current prices driving strong returns. The company has more than $60 million in cash, and it managed to pay off a staggering $125 million in debt.

That's with no lifting in Q4 2024 which will help the company in 2025.

BTG Integration

The company is roughly a week from closing its merger with BTG Oil & Gas.

The company will double its immediate FCF with BTG Oil and Gas receiving 35.5% of the outstanding share capital of the outstanding capital. That's enlarged from 35%, due to Africa Oil Corporation's repurchases of its shares. We'd like to see share repurchases continue in the upcoming years, and even ramp up with the company's FCF.

The company is moving to $100 million in annual dividends with a new combined $900 million market cap. The company's pro forma $0.15 annual dividend is a double-digit dividend yield as the base yield that the company can comfortably afford. The company will still have substantial FCF on top of that.

The company earned $68 million to shareholders in 2024, a double-digit return, and it's doubling FCF in 2025 with a much smaller enlarged share count.

Venus Oil

For the long-term the company also has an exciting development opportunity in Venus Oil.

The company has a net 4% interest in Block 2913B through its shareholding in Impact Oil & Gas. The field is expected to see the FID in a year, with 150k barrels / day in production. That's 6k barrels / day in attributable production to the company from the first development. With additional drilling and developments it will be essential for the company's portfolio.

While production is expecting to trend down from 2025-2027, it'll pick up some in 2028, and improve from there. TotalEnergies is covering the development costs, with a fully carry, and the field has billions of barrels of oil. The 4% attributable to Africa Oil Corporation is enough to support 10s of thousands of barrels alone, with Guyana style developments.

The company has even larger stakes in the South Africa Orange Basin, but development is continuing there. The company has the cash flow to run development with long-term potential here.

Future Shareholder Returns

The company has the ability to drive future shareholder returns. The company sits at a $900 million market cap. Simply based on 2024, the company will earn almost $400 million in FCF. Continuing to 2027, even with the planned production decline, the company will still have $250 million in FCF. The company has $350 million in debt through Prime, $275 million counting cash.

The company is committing to $100 million in dividends, which it can comfortably afford, but we'd like to see it match that with share buybacks. Regardless, the company can within a single year, pay its debt down to $0, and pay a double-digit shareholder return. For now, repurchases in 2025 are capped at $14-15 million.

We don't see another company that's as obviously undervalued.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is crude oil prices. The company is profitable at current levels. However, there's real recession risk in the economy, and there's pressure to increase production. That could push down prices, which could substantially hurt Africa Oil Corporation's ability to drive its future shareholder returns.

Conclusion

Africa Oil Corporation is about to wrap off its merger with BTG Group and initiate a double-digit dividend yield. That's a base dividend yield that the company can comfortably afford. The company is continuing to generate massive FCF, enough to comfortably handle its debt, pay dividends, and repurchase shares.

Putting all this, at its current share price, Africa Oil Corporation is a valuable investment. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Buy The Dip With 10% Yield: BrightSpire Capital
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

"Life is about timing"

– Carl Lewis

When planning to be defensive or offensive, timing can be massively important. Whenever we observe a sector in the stock market that is enduring a lot of negative sentiment or a sector that is benefiting from a ton of positive sentiment, the timing of management's actions can vastly change the outcome. Acting too soon in a defensive nature can cause you to lag behind your more aggressive peers. However, being aggressive for too long can cause you to have a cataclysmic fall to the other side, meaning that a defensive management team may not post the same stellar results but is less likely to post harmful results over the long run. A muted reaction from the market is usually caused by a conservative management team versus an excited reaction from the market due to those teams who are more aggressive, whether positive or negative.

The commercial mortgage REIT sector has been the source of drama this earnings season. We saw Apollo Commercial Real Estate (ARI) rally double digits as it produced an outlook for growing its portfolio and redeploying capital that was previously tied up in non-performing loans. Then we saw Ares Commercial Real Estate (ACRE) drop double digits as its management issued an outlook that it would take more time to deal with its defaults, and it didn't expect to grow its portfolio anytime soon, necessitating a dividend cut.

Today, I want to dive into the earnings of another commercial mREIT that we've covered before to see where their earnings landed them.

Let's dive in!

A Sector With Wide-Ranging Outcomes

BrightSpire Capital, Inc. (BRSP), yielding 10.3%, is a commercial mREIT with owns and originates a portfolio of loans or mortgages tied to commercial properties. The commercial real estate market has been hit hard by rising interest rates and reduced interest from large companies in owning or leasing properties when they became less economically attractive.

The earnings from BRSP when compared to ARI and ACRE were right in the middle. BRSP management warned us that adjusted distributable earnings would decline, and they did to $0.18/share. However, it appears the dividend was reduced enough and earnings continued to out-earn the dividend. Source

Book value took a large hit in Q2 2024, but has been relatively stable quarter to quarter since then.

Throughout 2024, BRSP's focus has been on maintaining a high level of liquidity while managing the loans on its watchlist. In Q4, we saw some progress with three loans coming off of the watchlist and one loan being added to it.

BRSP provides investors with significant details about the loans on their watchlist.

The largest loan by far is the hotel in San Jose in California. BRSP has filed paperwork to foreclose on the property, and the company that owns it is currently in bankruptcy court. That is good news because the owners have been working to find a new loan to refinance BRSP and a mezzanine loan owned by Hilton. There are news reports that a new lender has been found willing to refinance the entire mortgage. So while the loan is a 5 on their risk scale because it is in active foreclosure, there is a lot of reason to believe that BRSP will experience a full recovery of their capital on this loan.

In the earnings release, management commentary was short and sweet:

"Looking ahead, we expect to continue to resolve watchlist and REO assets and grow the portfolio and earnings through new loan originations."

On the origination front, BRSP originated $53 million in new loans.

That isn't a ton, considering they had $93 million in loans repaying, and in the last earnings call management suggested that they wanted to increase the overall size of the portfolio by approximately $1 billion in 2025. However, this should be viewed in the context that BRSP has not originated a new loan since Q4 2022. That's right, for two years BRSP has not been in the market making loans. It has been coasting on its prior portfolio which was $3.5 billion in loans and that portfolio has slowly shrunk over the past two years.

So coming from a complete dead stop, we can expect it to take time to ramp up their origination pipeline. The company noted in their subsequent events that they have originated another $53 million in loans so far in 2025. That means in Q1 we will see higher originations than in Q4.

BRSP pulled in their horns before everyone else did. Management allowed the portfolio to shrink from over $3.5 billion to about $2.5 billion. Now, they are originating enough so that the portfolio isn't shrinking. In the first half of 2025, we expect that BRSP's portfolio will be growing. Ultimately, that is a positive for distributable earnings and that will lead to the dividend growing again.

We expect that in Q1 management will keep the dividend where it is, and given management's history of being somewhat conservative with their payout ratio, it will likely remain flat for Q2 as well. It will be Q3 or Q4, where the portfolio is growing and BRSP is able to redeploy capital that is extracted from their watchlist loans that a return to dividend growth will be on the table.

This earnings likely won't be seen as a home run by the market. However, it is an inflection point for the company where they have turned from 100% defense to playing some offense. For years, BRSP has been hunkered down, waiting for economic conditions to change, and in Q4 they decided conditions changed enough to resume growth. BRSP is trading at a bargain price and investors should take advantage of it.

Conclusion

BRSP has decided to shift its footing.

BRSP has spent numerous quarters allowing its portfolio to wind down and standing in a purely defensive position. This allowed them to avoid many of the pitfalls their peers have fallen into by being overly aggressive during a period of sector-wide weakness. Now, they're in the process of ramping up their portfolio again. We're seeing a complete change in their focus from being completely defensive to being more aggressive in their lending. This will allow them to see their earnings ramp up. It's hard to generate the same level of earnings from a portfolio that's only $2.5 billion versus a portfolio that's $3.5 billion in value. This ramp-up is a benefit as they'll have the ability to cover their current dividend, as we've seen from these most recent earnings, and will continue to support it as they ramp up.

In retirement, you can unlock higher levels of income by investing a portion of your portfolio in down-trodden sectors. Often, they are undervalued and higher-yielding due to the negative sentiment that swirls around them. BRSP is a great example of a firm that's sold off with peers in sympathy but took a more defensive approach, allowing it to be on solid footing for the sector's recovery.
